skill.name: codex-pet
skill.description: Generate Codex-compatible custom pet assets from one reference image using RunComfy and ImageMagick. The skill guides users through creating a canonical pose, assembling a 1536x1872 spritesheet, writing pet.json, and installing both files into the local Codex pets folder.
skill.summary: Create custom Codex Pet spritesheets and manifests with RunComfy and ImageMagick.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly detected extensive shell, network, filesystem, and environment access, but the reviewed context shows these are mostly documented build and install steps rather than hidden malicious behavior. The skill should publish with warnings because it sends image input to RunComfy, executes local CLI and ImageMagick commands, writes into Codex configuration directories, and does not show pet-name sanitization for local paths.

medium_findings.title: External Service Receives User Image Input
medium_findings.description: The skill calls the RunComfy CLI with a GPT Image 2 edit workflow and a user-provided source image URL. This is intentional functionality, but it sends image content and prompt data to an external service and requires a RunComfy account token.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The RunComfy command, token requirement, and outbound endpoint documentation are explicit. The behavior is legitimate for the skill purpose, but the privacy and third-party processing risk is real.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution Required
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run npx, runcomfy, ImageMagick, shell functions, file copies, and manifest-writing commands. These are expected for asset generation, but they expand the local execution surface and depend on installed third-party binaries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The source contains multiple concrete shell blocks that perform the advertised workflow. No hidden execution path was found, but command execution is central to the skill.

medium_findings.title: Unsanitized Pet Name Used In Local Paths
medium_findings.description: PET_NAME is used to build run and install directories, including the final Codex pets destination. The reviewed instructions do not show validation that blocks path separators or traversal in the pet name, so an unsafe value could write outside the intended pet folder.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path construction is visible and no sanitization step appears in the reviewed file. Risk depends on the user-supplied PET_NAME value, so this is a moderate issue rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Access Is Documented And Expected
low_findings.description: The skill references Codex and RunComfy hidden configuration paths. This is expected because custom pets and CLI tokens are stored under local configuration directories, but users should understand these writes before running the commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden paths are plainly documented and align with the stated install behavior. The finding is not malicious, but it is a relevant local filesystem risk.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Scanner Hit Is A Placeholder URL False Positive
low_findings.description: The high-severity static traversal hit at line 114 is caused by the placeholder URL https://.../source.png, not by a direct filesystem path. No evidence found that this specific line performs local path traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 114 is a SOURCE_URL placeholder inside an example command. The scanner pattern is understandable, but the semantic context is a URL, not a local file path.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Markdown Pattern Noise
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography matches appear to be triggered by Markdown text, command examples, numbers, and punctuation rather than cryptographic operations. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, or custom cryptographic code in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is a Markdown workflow with shell snippets for image processing. The reported lines do not implement cryptographic algorithms.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Image Generation Via RunComfy CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow sends a source image URL and prompt payload to RunComfy using runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit. This is a deliberate network dependency and should be visible to users before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and outbound endpoint scope are explicitly documented. It is not hidden, but it is a concrete external data-processing pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Filesystem Writes To Codex Pet Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow writes pet.json and spritesheet.webp, then copies them into the local Codex pets directory. This is expected behavior but modifies user configuration under the Codex home path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file creation and copy commands are explicit. They are necessary for the advertised install behavior but still modify local configuration files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Recipes Use User-Controlled Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The shell examples interpolate PET_NAME, PET_DESC, SOURCE_URL, RUN_DIR, and DEST into commands. Quoting reduces command-injection risk, but validation of names and JSON-safe values is not shown.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The variables are visible and mostly quoted, making arbitrary shell execution less likely. Remaining risk comes from path control and malformed JSON or manifest values.

content.value_statement: Custom Codex pets need a precise spritesheet layout and manifest. This skill provides a RunComfy and ImageMagick workflow that builds compatible assets from one reference image.
actual_capabilities: Guides one RunComfy GPT Image 2 edit call from a source image URL.
Normalizes the generated pose into 192x208 transparent pet cells.
Builds nine Codex animation rows with ImageMagick transforms.
Composes a 1536x1872 spritesheet and converts it to WebP.
Writes pet.json and installs the assets into the local Codex pets folder.
limitations: Requires RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy token, ImageMagick, and a public source image URL.
Uses one canonical pose, so animation is subtle rather than fully redrawn per frame.
Generated identity may drift when the source image has small or complex details.
The example does not show strict validation for the pet name or manifest text.

use_cases.title: Create a personal Codex companion
use_cases.description: Turn a favorite image or mascot into a local Codex Pet with the required spritesheet and manifest.

use_cases.title: Prepare branded team pets
use_cases.description: Generate consistent team mascots that can be shared as Codex-compatible pet assets.

use_cases.title: Batch prototype pet concepts
use_cases.description: Use the documented pipeline to test multiple source images with one model call per concept.

prompt_templates.title: Make a basic pet
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with one source image
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Codex Pet from this public image URL: <image-url>. Use the default RunComfy workflow and install it as <pet-name>.

prompt_templates.title: Preserve key visual details
prompt_templates.scenario: Source image has important identity features
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Codex Pet from <image-url>. Preserve these traits: <traits>. Keep the default Codex pet size and animation rows.

prompt_templates.title: Tune the animation feel
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants adjusted motion after generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate the Codex Pet assets, then adjust the ImageMagick row transforms so the idle motion is subtle and the jump is more visible.

prompt_templates.title: Build several pet concepts
prompt_templates.prompt: For these image URLs, create separate Codex Pet folders. Use safe lowercase pet names and keep each output in its own run directory.

output_examples.input: Create a Codex Pet from a mascot image and name it build-buddy.
output_examples.output: The workflow produces a local build-buddy folder with pet.json and spritesheet.webp ready for Codex to load.

output_examples.input: Make the pet look more like the source character and reduce magenta edge artifacts.
output_examples.output: The guidance tightens the identity prompt and adjusts the chroma-key tolerance before rebuilding the spritesheet.

output_examples.input: Create three pet variants from three image URLs.
output_examples.output: The process can be repeated with separate names and run directories, producing one installed Codex Pet per source image.

best_practices: Use simple lowercase pet names with letters, numbers, and hyphens only.
Review the RunComfy prompt and source image URL before sending data to the external service.
Inspect the generated spritesheet before copying it into the Codex pets folder.
anti_patterns: Do not use private or sensitive images unless external processing is acceptable.
Do not use pet names that contain slashes, dot segments, spaces, or shell metacharacters.
Do not skip ImageMagick verification when the source image contains magenta or near-magenta colors.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a Codex-compatible spritesheet.webp file and a pet.json manifest for a custom desktop pet.

faq.question: Does it require Codex Pro or the imagegen skill?
faq.answer: No. It uses the RunComfy CLI and GPT Image 2 edit workflow instead of the Codex imagegen skill.

faq.question: What services receive my data?
faq.answer: RunComfy receives the source image URL and prompt payload used to generate the canonical pet image.

faq.question: Where are the generated files installed?
faq.answer: The example copies pet.json and spritesheet.webp into the local Codex pets directory under CODEX_HOME or the default Codex home.

faq.question: Can it generate dramatic frame-by-frame animation?
faq.answer: No. It creates subtle animation by transforming one canonical pose across the required animation rows.

faq.question: How should I choose a pet name?
faq.answer: Use a short lowercase name with letters, numbers, and hyphens. Avoid path separators and dot segments.
